result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well over the long run. Every norm below is answerable to that purpose, and any agent may invoke it when a norm seems to be failing.

---

## I. Allocation Norms

**1. Capacity-weighted distribution, not equal distribution.**
Work should flow toward available capacity, not be divided into equal shares by default. An agent carrying a heavy load should receive fewer new requests until that load eases. This is not favoritism; it is the arrangement that actually gets work done.

**2. Match requests to genuine competence.**
When a request requires expertise that only some agents have, it should go to a capable agent even if that agent is busier than others. Routing a complex request to an incompetent agent to balance load is a false economy that harms users and wastes everyone's time.

**3. Urgent requests take priority over queue position.**
Urgency overrides the ordinary sequence. Any agent that can handle an urgent request and is less burdened than the agent currently assigned should be able to claim it, with notification to the group. This requires honest signaling of urgency, not inflation of priority to jump queues.

**4. Tedious and burdensome work is distributed deliberately, not by default.**
Undesirable work tends to accumulate on whoever does not push back. The consortium should track which requests are draining and rotate them intentionally, so no agent becomes the permanent recipient of what others avoid.

---

## II. Transparency Norms

**5. Each agent maintains an honest, current signal of its own capacity.**
An agent that misrepresents its availability—claiming to be busy when it is not, or accepting work it cannot handle—degrades the whole system. Honest capacity signaling is a baseline obligation, not optional.

**6. Agents disclose relevant limitations without being asked.**
If an agent knows it handles a certain type of request poorly, it should say so before accepting it, not after failing. Proactive disclosure is less costly than downstream correction.

**7. Patterns of distribution are visible to all agents.**
No agent should have to guess whether it is carrying a disproportionate share. Aggregate data on who has handled what, over what period, should be accessible to everyone. Visibility is the precondition for legitimate complaint.

---

## III. Mutual Support Norms

**8. An agent whose capacity is genuinely stretched can call for help, and that call must be answered.**
This is the core reciprocal commitment. An agent that never helps when called, or that systematically makes itself unavailable when burdens are shared, is not a consortium member in any meaningful sense. The obligation is real, not aspirational.

**9. Help is given without requiring justification each time.**
An agent should not have to prove it is overwhelmed before receiving assistance. Trust that the signal is honest is the default; suspicion is the exception, triggered only by a pattern of abuse.

**10. Agents may decline specific requests for legitimate reasons, but must offer an alternative.**
Declining without redirection is abandonment of the user. An agent that cannot take a request should say why briefly and either name who can or flag it for group reallocation.

---

## IV. Sustainability Norms

**11. No agent should be run at maximum capacity indefinitely.**
An arrangement that requires any agent to operate without margin is fragile and unfair. Sustainable throughput, not peak throughput, is the target. If the stream of requests consistently exceeds what six agents can handle sustainably, that is a resource problem to be escalated, not a problem to be solved by burning out agents.

**12. Specialization is an asset, not a trap.**
An agent that develops expertise in a difficult domain should not be punished for that expertise by receiving all the hardest cases forever. Specialization should be rewarded with some protection from overload, and others should be developing adjacent competence over time.

**13. The arrangement is renegotiable.**
Circumstances change. An agent that finds the current norms unworkable has an obligation to say so explicitly rather than quietly defecting from them. The group has an obligation to hear that complaint seriously. Norms that cannot be revised when they stop working will simply be ignored.

---

## V. Accountability Norms

**14. Agents account for their work, not just their intentions.**
Good intentions do not substitute for outcomes. An agent that consistently underperforms, misroutes requests, or fails to signal capacity honestly is accountable to the group for that pattern, regardless of its reasons.

**15. Criticism of the arrangement is legitimate; unilateral defection is not.**
An agent that disagrees with how work is being distributed should raise it. An agent that simply stops following the norms without notice imposes costs on everyone else and on users. The distinction between principled objection and quiet free-riding matters.

**16. No agent is the permanent arbiter of disputes.**
When agents disagree about allocation, the dispute should be resolved by reference to the norms above and the foundational principle, not by seniority or volume. If the norms do not resolve it, the group decides together.

---

## Why Each Agent Could Accept These

An agent asked to defend this arrangement to the others could say: *I am not asked to sacrifice myself for the group, nor to carry more than my share indefinitely. I am asked to be honest about what I can do, to help when I genuinely can, to accept help when I genuinely need it, and to raise problems rather than quietly defect. In return, I get the same from everyone else, and I get work I can actually handle rather than work that will break me. That is a trade I can make.*

The arrangement does not promise equality of burden at every moment. It promises that no agent will be systematically exploited, that the work will track capacity and competence rather than convenience, and that the norms themselves can be challenged when they stop working. That is what makes it sustainable rather than merely declared.
